solutions.solution: Since P is older than S, and since R is older than V: 60 61 62 63 64 65 S Y P O S T So, if P is older than O, there is only one possible order in which the group could have been born. Q. 37-43: Begin by summarising the information: N < (J + O) J < M K < 3 There is one further conclusion that can be drawn. Since N is to be played before J and J before M, N must be played before M.

At the outset, we defined one further conclusion: that N must come before M. That the other choices are not logically necessary can be shown by citing the following possible order: I 2 3 4 5 6 N J K L M O

As for (1), J cannot be played last because J is played before M. As for (2), K must be played third, not first. As for (3), M cannot be played second, because (as we learned above) both J and N must be played before M. As for (4), it is K that must be played third<197>not N. (5), however, is a possibility, as shown by the diagram: I 2 3 4 5 6 N J K L M O

Enter the additional information in a diagram: I 2 3 4 5 6 L K N must be played before J and O and also M. And this means that N must be played second: I 2 3 4 5 6 L N K Then the order of the other three pieces is not fixed, but these further conclusions are sufficient to answer the question asked.

With K as the third piece, there is only one place to accommodate a series of three pieces: I 2 3 4 5 6 K L M O Then, since N comes before J, the order must be: I 2 3 4 5 6 N J K L M O So, J must be played second.

Enter the additional information on a diagram: I 2 3 4 5 6 K L Since N must be played before J, O and M, N must be first: I 2 3 4 5 6 N K L Now, given that J must precede M, there are only three possibilities: I 2 3 4 5 6 N J K M O L N J K O M L N O K J M L So, the first and second pieces could be N and J, or N and O.

N must be played before three other pieces (J, O and M), so given that K is played third N cannot be played later than second. So, N cannot be played after K. It is possible, however, to play the others immediately after K: I 2 3 4 5 6 N K J L M O or N J K L M O or N J K M L O or N K O M L

Here is another selection set. Begin by summarising the information: Q = Entertainment L = M J ≠ K M ≠ O So far, the first condition, that Q serves on the Entertainment Committee, has been set out. Using the second condition, that L and M must serve on the same committee (2). Using the third condition, that J cannot serve on the same committee, we eliminate (1). Finally, using that last condition, we eliminate the condition, therefore, the only acceptable committee listed is (5).

You may find it helpful to start with J and M together: If you put L and M on the Food Committee along with J (note that which places both N and R on the Games Committee), this violates the fourth condition. Since L and M cannot serve on different committees, this means L and M cannot both be on the Games Committee.
